retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Pathophysiology -- Spinal Cord Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Primary spinal cord injury occurs due to local spine deformation, such as direct compression. Secondary spinal cord injury occurs following primary injury and involves cascades of biochemical and cellular processes, including electrolyte disturbances, free radical damage, edema, ischemia, and inflammation. [25] Secondary spinal cord injury has several phases: acute, subacute, and chronic. During the acute phase (up to 48 hours after the primary injury), hemorrhage and ischemia lead to ion balance disruption, excitotoxicity, and inflammation. During the subacute phase (up to two weeks following primary injury), there is a phagocytic response and a reactive proliferation of astrocytes, which leads to a glial scar in the chronic phase. The thinking is that scarification is the critical component to permanent disability because it prevents axonal regeneration; axons otherwise could regenerate, but their growth is blocked. However, that notion has been challenged, and there are suggestions that astrocyte scar formation could aid in regeneration. [26] The scarification process is complete in the chronic phase (over six months after the primary injury). [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Pathophysiology -- SCI Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: SCIs arise from complex mechanisms, resulting in varying neurologic deficits depending on the location and extent of the injury. The mechanisms underlying SCIs are classified as either primary or secondary (see Table. SCI Phases). Secondary SCI emerges from a series of biological phenomena that begin within minutes of a primary injury and continue for weeks to months. The acute secondary injury phase encompasses vascular damage, ionic imbalances, free radical formation, the initial inflammatory response, and neurotransmitter accumulation, known as excitotoxicity. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Ischemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize recent evidence-based diagnostic criteria for acute spinal cord ischemia. Review current theories regarding the mechanism of spinal cord ischemia. Outline the best imaging tools to evaluate for spinal cord ischemia. Describe the role of interprofessional teams in collaborating to prognosticate recovery from spinal cord ischemia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Within the human body, there are 33 vertebrae and 31 spinal nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: PTS develops due to altered CSF dynamics, spinal cord ischemia, and progressive tissue degeneration following SCI. The primary mechanism involves obstruction of normal CSF flow, leading to fluid accumulation within the spinal cord parenchyma, ultimately forming a syrinx. This obstruction can arise from scar tissue, arachnoid adhesions, meningeal fibrosis, or spinal deformities such as posttraumatic kyphosis and stenosis. Over time, the trapped CSF expands the syrinx, compressing surrounding neural structures and causing progressive neurological deterioration.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Pathophysiology -- Post-SCI Immune Response
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is protracted in the subacute and chronic phases. The inflammatory cell composition and phenotype vary with the stage of inflammation and the signal molecules present within the injury microenvironment. T and B cells, microglia, and macrophages can adopt a proinflammatory, anti-inflammatory, or proregenerative phenotype. Spinal cord disruption results in deficits of motor and sensory function below the level of the injury. Disability patterns depend on the injury level and extent of spinal tract involvement. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Incomplete Spinal Cord Injuries (SCIs)
retrieved_docs.content: Central cord syndrome [8] Most common incomplete SCI Pathophysiology: central gray matter injury Mechanism(s): hyperextension (ie, from a fall) in a patient with underlying cervical spinal canal stenosis Clinical presentation: Upper extremity loss of motor function (lower extremity motor function not affected/minimally affected) Sensory sparing variable Prognosis: Good

retrieved_docs.title: Axonotmesis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding the end organs, the remaining motor units on muscles increase in size with hypertrophy of the innervated muscle fibers. A pruning process later takes place when axons receiving inadequate neurotrophic factors diminish. This process begins within 4 days following the insult with healing requiring 3 to 6 months. [12] Pre-ganglionic avulsion injuries with the discontinuity of the spinal roots to the central cord lack regenerative neurons, unlike in postganglionic injuries distal to the proximal stump. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The use of steroids in the treatment of spinal cord injuries remains a topic of ongoing debate. Steroids were initially thought to enhance anti-inflammatory mechanisms, reduce secondary SCI, and increase cell viability. The results of initial studies suggested potential benefits from steroid administration, yet subsequent research has failed to substantiate any such advantage. The latest guidelines, published in 2017, recommend considering a 24-hour high-dose methylprednisolone infusion for adult patients within 8 hours following acute spinal cord injury. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Shock -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying molecular mechanisms for spinal shock remain largely unknown. Animal models have been used to study the pathophysiology of spinal shock, which elucidates the natural progression of injury in SCIs to some degree. After initial hemorrhagic foci in the gray matter, there appears to be significant protein accumulation in the gray matter of the spinal cord. Edema then ensues and peaks 3 to 6 days after the initial injury and can be visualized on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for up to 2 weeks. The slow process of central cord necrosis and vacuolization subsequently occurs, continuing for about 2 months. The characteristically thin rim of white matter surrounding the central core of necrosis remains intact throughout this process. Often, patients with SCIs begin losing neurologic function above the level of the lesion several days after the initial insult, primarily due to spinal cord pathway rearrangement. Once this process abates, normal function above the injury returns, although the exact time needed for this process has not been defined and may last from weeks to months. If a patient survives the initial injury but remains immobile, the area fills with gliotic tissue. [13] The transection of the spinal cord results in spinal shock and transient extinction of reflexes below the level of injury. The main pathophysiological mechanisms include: [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The syrinx cavity is typically lined by reactive astrocytes rather than a true epithelial lining, distinguishing it from congenital syringomyelia. The surrounding spinal cord tissue often exhibits a combination of demyelination, axonal loss, and necrosis, indicative of progressive damage due to chronic compression and ischemia. [14] Gliosis is a prominent feature, particularly at the margins of the syrinx, where reactive astrocytes form a glial scar. This scarring contributes to further obstruction of CSF flow and limits the potential for neural regeneration. The perisyringeal tissue often shows evidence of chronic inflammation, with infiltration of lymphocytes and macrophages. Microglial activation is frequently observed, further contributing to ongoing neuroinflammation and tissue degradation.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Most clinical trials focus on this therapy due to the easier MSC preparation and regulatory compliance. In contrast, loading therapy employs stem cells capable of producing neural cells, such as olfactory ensheathing cells and neural progenitor cells, and stem cells derived from embryonic stem cells. Engraftment of these cells has a greater likelihood of replacing lost nerve cells in terms of functionality, though the procedure requires invasive transplantation methods and complex cell preparation processes. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemia-reperfusion injury following SCI leads to microvascular damage, promoting chronic inflammation and gliotic scarring, which further disrupts CSF circulation and contributes to syrinx formation. Biomechanical alterations in the injured spinal cord, such as tethering from posttraumatic fibrosis, exert longitudinal traction on the cord and exacerbate syrinx expansion. Persistent low-grade inflammation in the injured spinal cord environment leads to extracellular matrix remodeling and barrier dysfunction, allowing fluid accumulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Axon -- Pathophysiology -- Axonal acute damage
retrieved_docs.content: Apart from MS, research focuses on understanding the mechanisms of axonal damage in acute and chronic pathologies. In the mid-nineteenth century, the British physiologist Augustus Volney Waller (1816-1870) showed that a full-thickness section of a nerve induces degeneration and reabsorption of its distal segment. The leading cause of this degeneration (Wallerian degeneration) is the interruption of axonal flow and the lack of ribosomes in the axon. The post-injury degenerative process has been subject to further dissection. In the case of a focal traumatic axonal lesion (e.g., due to spinal cord injury, SCI), the adjacent 400 to 600 micrometers of the axon on both sides of the lesion is involved in a rapid degenerative process (acute axonal degeneration). The molecular mechanisms comprise a cascade of events involving a rapid calcium influx into the axon, activation of calcium-dependent proteases (e.g., calpain and calcineurin), mitochondrial damage with the production of reactive oxygen species, alterations of neurofilaments with the fragmentation of microtubules, and impairment of axonal transport. The cascade culminates in the activation of autophagy. After 24 to 72 hours, the distal part of the axon is subject to Wallerian degeneration, which is mediated by the activation of nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase (neuroprotective under physiological conditions) and is directed along the axon. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 604
retrieved_docs.content: External cause codes: Firearm are classified as Injury due to legal intervention by firearm. Injury due to legal intervention by firearm is associated with the morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion also has the associated morphology تَلَف الأنسجة, indicating that tissue damage is a common pathological feature of both firearm‑related legal intervention injuries and cervical spine fractures with spinal cord involvement. The tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), suggesting that the morphological consequence of tissue injury may involve neurotoxic processes. Recognizing these connections helps clinicians understand injury mechanisms, anticipate tissue and neural complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2194
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is linked to تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage), and this tissue damage is further associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). Consequently, a cervical spine fracture that involves the spinal cord can lead to direct tissue injury that may produce neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of addressing both structural damage and its potential neurotoxic consequences in patient management.

Additional chronic outcomes include late effects of multiple‑bone fractures, spine/trunk fractures without spinal cord lesion, and fractures of the lower limbs. These relationships illustrate how traumatic mechanisms (e.g., accidental contact, crushing injury), specific fracture types, intracranial injuries, and their acute management are interconnected with a comprehensive hierarchy of region‑specific and generic late‑effect outcomes, as well as with behavioral injury categories captured by the SMQ framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, اعتلال عصبي عضلي, التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية. Each of these entities is classified under Other disorders of nervous system, reflecting their shared involvement of the nervous system despite differing pathophysiological mechanisms. For example, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث represent distinct forms of neuropathy, while اعتلال عصبي عضلي denotes a neuromuscular disorder. Inflammatory respiratory involvement is captured by التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, whereas الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي describes a reflexive degenerative process affecting the lower limb. Dاء شاركو‑ماري‑توث refers to a specific neurodegenerative disease, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية characterizes a syndrome combining spinal cord injury–related motor deficits with neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Recognizing that all these conditions are grouped under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients presenting with complex neurological presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: legal interventions, cervical spine fractures, cutting/piercing mechanisms, and neurotoxic tissue damage through shared classifications, hierarchical inverse‑isa and cause‑of relationships, and common morphological outcomes such as تَلَف الأنسجة, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for coding, diagnosing, and managing a wide spectrum of injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal cord injury is classified as an avulsion injury (تمزق جافوي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 811
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is identified as a specific type of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية (cervical vertebral fracture) and is characterized by the presence of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). This tissue damage is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that the morphological injury may involve toxic effects on neural structures. In the broader classification scheme, other fractures are grouped under the category of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, emphasizing the relevance of this injury pattern within orthopedic taxonomy. Clinically, كسر الحوض (pelvic fracture) frequently co‑occurs with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, highlighting the need for comprehensive assessment of patients with multiple skeletal injuries. Additionally, an injury of uncertain intent resulting from an aircraft crash is described as having تَلَف الأنسجة as its associated morphology, reinforcing the connection between traumatic mechanisms and tissue damage. Together, these relationships underscore the interdependence of cervical spine fractures, associated tissue injury, neurotoxic effects, and concurrent pelvic fractures in the evaluation and management of complex trauma patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1652
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion and the injury whose intent is undetermined but resulted from an aircraft crash are both described as having an associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that the tissue damage observed in these severe injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. This interconnected understanding highlights that both the cervical spine fracture with cord involvement and the ambiguous aircraft‑related injury share a common pathological feature of tissue damage, which can contribute to neurotoxic effects that are relevant for diagnosis, management, and prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc procedures are associated with the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية). Post‑laminectomy syndrome is classified as a subtype of spine disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري). This relationship highlights that patients undergoing laminectomy or disc excision may experience persistent pain and functional impairment characteristic of this spinal disorder, underscoring the need for careful postoperative management and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: these interconnections demonstrate how peripheral‑nerve pathology, entrapment‑syndrome decompression, pain and migraine mechanisms, and musculoskeletal disorders converge in neurological and musculoskeletal patient care, guiding diagnosis, therapeutic planning, and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

Collectively, these relationships depict “Other disorders of nervous system” as an integrative framework that connects diverse neuropathic, vascular, inflammatory, toxic, traumatic, hereditary, and musculoskeletal entities to their anatomical sites, diagnostic findings (including abnormal brain evoked‑potential maps), and therapeutic interventions, thereby guiding clinicians in the comprehensive recognition and management of nervous‑system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 200

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.
